meta.title: SF-SPAA-6: Access Governance, Cost Controls, and Operational Administration

questions.stem: Which system role is intended for creating and managing users and roles in a Snowflake account?

options.explanation: USERADMIN is the system role dedicated to user and role administration. **A** manages objects, **C** is granted broadly and is not an admin role, and **D** is organization-scoped rather than the standard account role for user management. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: A company wants separate billing and administration boundaries for two business units that still belong to one Snowflake organization. What is the best design?

options.explanation: Accounts provide isolated administration, billing, and security boundaries inside one organization. **A**, **B**, and **D** remain within one account boundary. [Introduction to Organizations | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/organizations)

questions.stem: Which command changes the active role for the current Snowflake session?

options.explanation: `USE ROLE` changes the session context. The other forms are not the standard Snowflake command. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: An administrator wants a custom role hierarchy that follows Snowflake best practice. Which TWO statements are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Custom roles should typically roll up to SYSADMIN
options.explanation: Snowflake recommends granting custom object-management roles into the SYSADMIN hierarchy. **C** is correct because role inheritance flows upward from granted roles to containing roles. **B** is incorrect because PUBLIC should not own production objects. **D** is incorrect because roles with the right privileges such as SECURITYADMIN can grant roles. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: A role can inherit privileges from roles granted to it
Only ACCOUNTADMIN can grant custom roles to other roles

questions.stem: A team needs a parameter change to apply to every new session unless a specific warehouse overrides it. Which parameter hierarchy explains this behavior most accurately?

options.text: Schema overrides warehouse, which overrides account
User overrides account, but warehouses cannot override parameters
Warehouse can override account for warehouse-scoped execution settings
options.explanation: Snowflake supports parameter hierarchies, and many execution settings can be set at account level then overridden at warehouse level. **A** is not the relevant hierarchy for warehouse execution settings, **B** is too narrow, and **D** is false because object-level overrides exist. [Parameters](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters)

options.text: Only session-level SET commands can override account parameters

questions.stem: What action can a resource monitor take when a credit threshold is reached?

options.explanation: Resource monitors can notify, suspend, or suspend immediately when thresholds are reached. **A**, **C**, and **D** are outside resource monitor behavior. [Working with resource monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A warehouse should stop only after currently running statements finish when a monthly quota is consumed. Which resource monitor action fits best?

options.explanation: `SUSPEND` prevents new queries while allowing running statements to finish. **A** only notifies, **C** can stop immediately, and **D** is not the documented action. [Working with resource monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: Which view is designed to analyze warehouse credit consumption over time in the shared ACCOUNT_USAGE schema?

options.explanation: `WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY` exposes credit usage by warehouse over time. **A** is for data access auditing, **C** for logins, and **D** for storage rather than compute credits. [Account Usage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: A FinOps lead wants better attribution of Snowflake cost to pipelines. Which TWO practices help most directly? (Select TWO)

options.text: Set query tags for pipeline jobs
options.explanation: Query tags help identify workload ownership. **B** is correct because Snowflake usage views support cost attribution analysis. **C** does not improve attribution, and **D** violates least privilege without improving chargeback quality. [Understanding overall cost](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/cost-understanding-overall)

options.text: Review QUERY_ATTRIBUTION_HISTORY or warehouse metering views
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to each pipeline owner

questions.stem: A warehouse experiences short bursts of concurrency during business hours but is idle overnight. Which configuration usually improves concurrency without keeping extra clusters running all night?

options.text: Enable multi-cluster and set auto-suspend appropriately
options.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses handle bursts, and auto-suspend limits idle spend. **B** wastes credits, **C** addresses sharing use cases, and **D** does not replace interactive warehouse concurrency. [Multi-cluster warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster)

options.text: Disable auto-suspend and size up permanently
Convert all queries to serverless tasks

questions.stem: Which privilege allows a role to grant or revoke privileges on an object it does not own when acting centrally?

options.explanation: `MANAGE GRANTS` enables centralized privilege administration. **A** and **C** are narrower object operations, and **D** is unrelated. [Configuring access control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure)

questions.stem: A role needs to automatically receive SELECT on future tables created in a schema. Which approach should be used?

options.text: Create a future grant on tables in the schema
options.explanation: Future grants can automatically apply privileges such as SELECT to new tables in a schema. **A** is too broad, **C** is insufficient for table access, and **D** is unrelated. [Configuring access control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure)

options.text: Grant USAGE on the warehouse only

questions.stem: In a managed access schema, who controls object privilege grants by default?

options.text: The schema owner or a role with MANAGE GRANTS
options.explanation: Managed access centralizes grant management with the schema owner or a role that has `MANAGE GRANTS`. **A** is true for regular schemas, not managed access. **C** and **D** are incorrect restrictions. [Configuring access control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure)

options.text: Only the object creator for the first 24 hours

questions.stem: A reporting role must query tables in `finance.mart` with least privilege. Which TWO grants are required at minimum before table-level SELECT becomes usable? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: A role needs database usage. **C** is correct because the role also needs schema usage before object privileges can be exercised. **A** uses non-Snowflake terminology, and **D** is far beyond least privilege. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: What is the main risk of transferring OWNERSHIP on a table to another role without copying current grants?

options.text: Existing privileges can be revoked during ownership transfer
options.explanation: Ownership transfer can revoke existing grants unless handled carefully with the supported grant-copy behavior. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: What does a Snowflake network policy primarily control?

options.text: Which client IP addresses can connect
options.explanation: Network policies restrict login access by allowed or blocked IP ranges. **A**, **C**, and **D** are not network policy functions. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A security team wants stricter restrictions for one privileged user than the rest of the account. Which policy assignment takes precedence for that user?

options.text: The user-level network policy applies to that user
options.explanation: A user-level network policy can be applied to an individual user and governs that user specifically. **A**, **C**, and **D** do not match Snowflake policy behavior. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

options.text: Network policies cannot be assigned to users

questions.stem: Why do many enterprises use PrivateLink or Private Endpoint with Snowflake?

options.text: To convert warehouses into multi-cluster mode
To avoid public internet exposure for connectivity
options.explanation: Private connectivity keeps traffic off the public internet. **A**, **C**, and **D** describe unrelated features. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

options.text: To replace RBAC with network-based authorization
To enable Time Travel for transient tables

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about network policies are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: They can use allow lists and block lists of IP addresses
options.explanation: Network policies evaluate allowed and blocked IP lists. **C** is correct because they affect whether authentication is allowed from a client address. **B** confuses network controls with RBAC, and **D** is incorrect because MFA is configured separately. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

options.text: They can grant table privileges when attached to a role

questions.stem: A company assigns an account-level network policy and later also assigns a user-level policy to a contractor. What is the main administrative benefit of the user-level assignment?

options.text: It lets the contractor bypass all IP restrictions
It allows a tighter exception without changing the whole account policy
options.explanation: A user-level policy supports a targeted exception or tighter restriction for one identity. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated or unsafe. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: Which table type does NOT have Fail-safe but can still support Time Travel?

options.explanation: Transient tables omit Fail-safe while still supporting limited Time Travel. **A** includes Fail-safe, and **C** and **D** are different storage models rather than the standard answer to this retention distinction. [Understanding and viewing Fail-safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: A team wants session-scoped scratch data that disappears automatically and is invisible to other sessions. Which table type is best?

options.explanation: Temporary tables are session-specific and automatically dropped at session end. **A** and **B** persist beyond the session, and **D** is not the correct standard table category here. [Understanding & using Time Travel](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel)

questions.stem: Which warehouse behavior helps reduce credit use for sporadic ad hoc workloads?

options.explanation: Auto-suspend and auto-resume prevent paying for idle warehouse time. **A** increases idle spend, **C** may raise cost, and **D** addresses storage, not compute idleness. [Overview of warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

options.text: Force all queries onto a larger warehouse

questions.stem: Which TWO characteristics are true of Snowflake multi-cluster warehouses? (Select TWO)

options.text: They add clusters to handle concurrency
options.explanation: Extra clusters can be added for concurrent demand. **C** is correct because this reduces queueing during spikes. **B** confuses concurrency with individual-query size, and **D** is incorrect because auto-suspend is still useful. [Multi-cluster warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster)

options.text: They are mainly for a single long-running query
They can reduce queueing during bursty workloads
They eliminate the need for auto-suspend

questions.stem: Why might an administrator lower `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS` on transient staging objects?

options.text: To reduce storage cost associated with retained history
options.explanation: Shorter retention can reduce storage retained for historical versions. **A** is the opposite, and **C** and **D** are unrelated. [Understanding & using Time Travel](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes warehouse resizing in Snowflake?

options.text: Resizing changes compute capacity independently of storage
options.explanation: Snowflake separates storage from compute, so warehouse size affects compute resources, not table storage layout directly. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. [Overview of warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

options.text: Resizing permanently changes database failover settings
Resizing is only available for serverless features

questions.stem: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view is most appropriate for reviewing login attempts and client connection details?

options.explanation: `LOGIN_HISTORY` records authentication events and client details. **A** covers queries, **C** covers object access, and **D** covers tags. [Account Usage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: Which view helps determine which tables were read by a specific statement for audit purposes?

options.explanation: `ACCESS_HISTORY` tracks data access details by query. **A** is for login events, **B** for statement metadata without the same object-level audit depth, and **D** is not the right audit source. [Account Usage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: A compliance officer wants to know who changed a masking policy definition yesterday. Which general Snowflake evidence source is most appropriate first?

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE views and relevant object history
options.explanation: Administrative and usage history views are the right starting point for auditing configuration changes. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not provide the same governance record. [Account Usage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: Which TWO practices strengthen Snowflake audit readiness? (Select TWO)

options.text: Review access and login history regularly
options.explanation: Regular review of access and login data supports incident response. **C** is correct because query tags improve traceability. **B** weakens security, and **D** is not an audit strategy. [Account Usage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN broadly so more people can investigate incidents
Use consistent query tagging for important workloads
Rely only on warehouse size changes as evidence

questions.stem: Which organization-level capability is unique to ORGADMIN compared with standard account roles?

options.text: Creating new databases in an account
Creating and viewing accounts in the organization
options.explanation: ORGADMIN operates at the organization level for account management. **A**, **C**, and **D** are standard account activities. [Introduction to Organizations | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/organizations)

questions.stem: What is the main difference between `SUSPEND` and `SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE` actions on a resource monitor?

options.text: `SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE` can stop running statements instead of waiting for completion
options.explanation: `SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE` can halt running work, whereas `SUSPEND` waits for active statements to complete. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. [Working with resource monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

questions.stem: A role has USAGE on a database and SELECT on a table but still gets an error when querying it. What privilege is most likely missing?

options.text: USAGE on the schema containing the table
options.explanation: Table access also requires schema usage. **B** is not enough for reading the object, **C** is unnecessary, and **D** is unrelated. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which scenario is the best fit for an account-level network policy?

options.text: Restrict all users to known corporate egress IP ranges
options.explanation: Account-level network policies set a broad authentication boundary for the account. **B**, **C**, and **D** are different governance features. [Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

options.text: Grant row-level security on a finance table
Charge back warehouse credits to teams
Create future grants on new schemas

questions.stem: Which storage object choice is usually most appropriate for persistent production data that needs the full maximum data protection features?

options.explanation: Permanent tables support the full standard protection model including Fail-safe. **A** and **B** reduce persistence or protection, and **D** is not a production table type. [Understanding and viewing Fail-safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: An auditor asks for evidence of which role executed a suspicious query and which warehouse it used. Which source should you inspect first?

options.explanation: `QUERY_HISTORY` contains statement-level execution metadata such as user, role context, and warehouse. The others are storage or replication usage views. [Account Usage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage)

questions.stem: A new administrator assumes ACCOUNTADMIN should own all objects to simplify operations. Why is this a poor practice?

options.text: It violates least privilege and increases blast radius for routine work
options.explanation: Using ACCOUNTADMIN broadly expands risk and goes against Snowflake least-privilege guidance. **A**, **C**, and **D** are false. [Overview of Access Control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

options.text: Objects owned by ACCOUNTADMIN cannot be queried
Snowflake disallows object ownership by ACCOUNTADMIN

questions.stem: Which object is the billing target of a warehouse-level resource monitor?

options.text: The users assigned to the warehouse
The credit usage consumed by that warehouse
options.explanation: Resource monitors track and act on credit consumption. **A**, **C**, and **D** are not their billing target. [Working with resource monitors](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors)

options.text: The data retention period on its tables
The number of rows loaded into schemas

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about future grants are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: They can reduce repetitive privilege administration on new objects
options.explanation: Future grants automate recurring grants. **C** is correct because they help when new objects are created regularly. **B** is incorrect because they are not retroactive for all existing objects, and **D** is incorrect because parent container usage privileges are still required. [Configuring access control](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure)

options.text: They apply retroactively to all existing objects automatically
They are useful for schemas that receive new tables frequently
They remove the need for USAGE privileges on parent containers

questions.stem: A warehouse serving dashboard queries is frequently idle for 12 minutes between morning refresh bursts. Which setting is most likely to reduce waste without harming usability?

options.explanation: Shortening auto-suspend cuts idle compute cost while auto-resume restores usability. **B** would hurt usability, and **C** and **D** do not address idle warehouse cost. [Overview of warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)
